title: Northern Ontario Energy Credit Questions and Answers - Canada.ca
content:
## Northern Ontario Energy Credit Questions and Answers
The questions and answers below refer to claims for the 2026 NOEC, which you apply for on your 2025 income tax and benefit return. However, the eligibility rules for previous years are similar to the rules for the 2026 NOEC. If you need these rules, when reading the questions and answers below, replace current year references with the previous year(s). For example, for the 2025 NOEC, replace 2025 with 2024, 2026 with 2025 and 2027 with 2026.
- What is the Northern Ontario energy credit (NOEC)?
- What areas of Ontario are considered Northern Ontario for the NOEC?
- Am I eligible for the 2026 NOEC?
- How do I apply for the 2026 NOEC?
- I am married (or living in a common-law relationship). Who should apply for the 2026 NOEC?
- How much is the 2026 NOEC?
- Do I have to calculate the 2026 NOEC?
- When will I get my 2026 NOEC payments?
- Who will issue the 2026 NOEC payments?
- What is a principal residence?
- What is eligible rent paid and what should I enter beside box 61100 on Form ON-BEN ?
- What is eligible property tax and what should I enter beside box 61120 on Form ON-BEN ?
- What amounts cannot be included as rent or property tax paid?
- What should I enter beside box 61210 on Form ON-BEN for my home energy costs paid on a reserve in Ontario for 2025?
- What should I enter beside box 61230 on Form ON-BEN for the amounts I paid for living in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Ontario for 2025?
- Do I have to provide my receipts with my 2025 return to prove the amounts I paid?
- My spouse owns our home but they are living in a nursing home. Are we entitled to the 2026 NOEC?
- My spouse and I pay property taxes on both our house and our cottage. When we calculate our property tax paid, can we add together the amounts we paid for both properties?
- In the year of death, is the estate eligible for any NOEC payment(s) for that year?
- How will the Canada Revenue Agency determine if I am a single parent so that I will qualify for the family-based 2026 NOEC payments?
- My ex-spouse and I share custody of our child. Neither of us has a new spouse or common-law partner. Will I receive the 2026 NOEC payments for my child?
- I'm single and I applied for the 2026 NOEC on my 2025 tax return. I still live in Northern Ontario and I gave birth to my first child on August 2, 2026. I do not share the care of my child. Will I qualify for the family-based NOEC payments?
- How do I register my child for the Canada child benefit (CCB) so that I will get the family-based 2026 NOEC payments?
- I filed for bankruptcy in 2025. Can I apply for the 2026 NOEC payments?
- I immigrated from outside of Canada to Northern Ontario on June 15, 2025. Can I apply for the 2026 NOEC payments?
- I have not filed my 2025 income tax and benefit return. Will I receive a 2026 NOEC payment?
- I applied for the 2026 NOEC on my 2025 return and met all of the conditions to get these payments. However, I have not received any payments—what should I do?
- I am eligible for the 2026 NOEC payments but I forgot to apply for them when I filed my 2025 income tax and benefit return. Can I still apply for them?
- I moved to Northern Ontario on January 5, 2026. Am I eligible for the 2026 NOEC payments?
- I moved out of Northern Ontario on August 15, 2026. Am I eligible for the 2026 NOEC payments?
- Do I have to be a Canadian citizen in order to be eligible for the 2026 NOEC?
- We are planning to sell our home in 2026. Are we still eligible for the 2026 NOEC?
- Are secondary residences in Northern Ontario, such as cottages, eligible for the NOEC?
- Can the NOEC be claimed on more than one principal residence in a tax year?
- My address recently changed—what should I do?
- I have an outstanding debt with the Canada Revenue Agency and didn't receive my goods and services tax credit because I owed money to the Government. Will I receive the NOEC?
- Is the NOEC a taxable benefit?
- Will there be interest paid or charged on any NOEC underpayments or overpayments?
- If my application for the NOEC is denied, can I appeal?
- Who should I contact if I have a question about the Ontario provincial government policy regarding the NOEC?
- If, on your 2025 return, you chose to wait to get a lump sum payment of your 2026 OTB entitlement and your annual entitlement is over $500, it will be issued on June 10, 2027. See question 9 under Ontario trillium benefit for more details.
- If your 2026 OTB annual entitlement is $500 or less, it will be issued in one lump-sum payment in the first payment month (usually July).
- If your 2025 return is assessed after June 19, 2026, see question 8 for when you can expect to receive your payment.
- If, on December 31, 2025, you were 64 years of age or older, and you owned and occupied a principal residence, or have a life lease or a lease of 10 or more years that is paid in full, in Ontario for which you or someone on your behalf paid property tax for 2025, you may also be eligible for the 2026 Ontario senior homeowners' property tax grant .
- You may also be eligible for the 2026 Ontario energy and property tax credit (OEPTC) if you lived in Ontario on December 31, 2025, and at least one of the following applies: rent or property tax for your principal residence in Ontario was paid by or for you for 2025 accommodation costs for living in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Ontario were paid by or for you for 2025 home energy costs (for example, electricity, heat) for your principal residence on a reserve were paid by or for you in Ontario for 2025 or you lived in a designated university, college, or private school residence in Ontario in 2025
- rent or property tax for your principal residence in Ontario was paid by or for you for 2025
- accommodation costs for living in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Ontario were paid by or for you for 2025
- home energy costs (for example, electricity, heat) for your principal residence on a reserve were paid by or for you in Ontario for 2025 or
- you lived in a designated university, college, or private school residence in Ontario in 2025

## 2. What areas of Ontario are considered Northern Ontario for the NOEC?
For the NOEC, Northern Ontario means the districts of Algoma, Cochrane, Kenora, Manitoulin, Nipissing, Parry Sound, Rainy River, Sudbury (including the City of Greater Sudbury), Thunder Bay, and Timiskaming. You must live in one of these areas on December 31, 2025, to be eligible for the 2026 NOEC.

## 3. Am I eligible for the 2026 NOEC?
You may be eligible for the 2026 NOEC, if:
- you were a resident of Northern Ontario (see question 2 ) on December 31, 2025, and one of the following conditions applies: you will be 18 years of age or older before June 1, 2027 you had a spouse or common-law partner on or before December 31, 2025 or you are a parent who lives or previously lived with your child (see question 20 ) and
- you will be 18 years of age or older before June 1, 2027
- you had a spouse or common-law partner on or before December 31, 2025 or
- you are a parent who lives or previously lived with your child (see question 20 ) and
- for 2025, at least one of the following conditions applies to you: rent for your principal residence (see question 10 ) in Northern Ontario, which was subject to Ontario municipal or school property tax, was paid by or for you property tax for your principal residence in Northern Ontario was paid by or for you you lived on a reserve in Northern Ontario and home energy costs (for example, electricity, heat) for your principal residence on the reserve were paid by or for you or you lived in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Northern Ontario and an amount for accommodation was paid by or for you
- rent for your principal residence (see question 10 ) in Northern Ontario, which was subject to Ontario municipal or school property tax, was paid by or for you
- property tax for your principal residence in Northern Ontario was paid by or for you
- you lived on a reserve in Northern Ontario and home energy costs (for example, electricity, heat) for your principal residence on the reserve were paid by or for you or
- you lived in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Northern Ontario and an amount for accommodation was paid by or for you
You must be a resident of Northern Ontario at the beginning of a month to receive that month’s payment.
You are not eligible for a particular monthly payment if you were confined to a prison or a similar institution for a period of 90 days or more that includes the first day of the payment month.
If you will turn 18 before June 1, 2027, and you meet the eligibility criteria, you should apply for the 2026 NOEC on your 2025 income tax and benefit return. You will only be issued the monthly payments for the periods after you turn 18. For example, if you turn 18 years of age on May 15, 2027, you will only be eligible for the 2026 NOEC payment that is issued on June 10, 2027.

## 4. How do I apply for the 2026 NOEC?
To apply for the 2026 NOEC, you must file a 2025 income tax and benefit return. You also must complete Form ON-BEN , Application for the 2026 Ontario Trillium Benefit and Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant . Be sure to attach the completed form to your return. This form is included in the tax package for residents of Ontario.
When completing Form ON-BEN, be sure to place a tick beside box 61040 in the section called "Application for the Northern Ontario energy credit (NOEC)." Complete Parts A and B, and, if applicable, Part C on the back of the form. Make sure you provide the following information for 2025, if applicable:
- enter the rent paid by or for you for your principal residence (including a private long-term care home) in Ontario for 2025 beside box 61100 (see question 11 and question 13 )
- enter the property tax paid by or for you (see note below) for your principal residence in Ontario for 2025 beside box 61120 (see question 12 and question 13 )
- enter your home energy costs (for example, electricity, heat) paid by or for you for your principal residence on a reserve in Ontario for 2025 beside box 61210 (see question 14 ) or
- enter the accommodation costs paid by or for you for living in a public or non-profit long term care home in Ontario for 2025 beside box 61230 (see question 15 )
If your municipality let you defer paying all or some of your 2025 property tax, you should enter only the amount of property tax actually paid to the municipality for the year.

## 5. I am married (or living in a common-law relationship). Who should apply for the 2026 NOEC?
If you were married or living in a common-law relationship on December 31, 2025, either one of you can apply for the 2026 NOEC for both of you. However, if only one spouse or common-law partner is 64 years of age or older on December 31, 2025, that spouse or common-law partner must apply for both of you.
The same spouse or common-law partner who applies for the 2026 Ontario senior homeowners' property tax grant (by ticking box 61070 on Form ON-BEN) and the 2026 Ontario energy and property tax credit (by ticking box 61020) must claim the 2026 NOEC and provide the other spouse's or common-law partner's net income in the "Identification" area on page 1 of the 2025 return.

## 6. How much is the 2026 NOEC?
If you are eligible for NOEC, the amount you receive depends on a number of factors including your adjusted family net income, your marital status and whether you have children.
The maximum 2026 NOEC is $189 for single individuals with no children and $290 for couples and single parents. If you are a single individual with no children, your credit will be reduced by 1% of your adjusted net income over $50,833. If you are a family, your credit will be reduced by 1% of your adjusted family net income over $65,356.

## 7. Do I have to calculate the 2026 NOEC?
No, you do not have to calculate your entitlement. After you apply, the 2026 NOEC will be calculated for you and, if you are entitled, your payments will be issued as explained in question 8 . If you are not entitled, in most cases, you will receive a notice explaining why.
If you would like to calculate your 2026 payment, see the 2026 Northern Ontario energy credit calculation sheets or use our on-line Child and family benefits calculator .
For the prior-year calculation sheets, go to Northern Ontario energy credit calculation sheets .

## 8. When will I get my 2026 NOEC payments?
Your NOEC entitlement will be added to your entitlement for the Ontario sales tax credit and/or the OEPTC, if applicable, and you will receive a combined payment, called the Ontario trillium benefit (OTB). The annual OTB entitlement is usually divided by 12 and payments are issued monthly (see below for more details and the note for exceptions).
If you meet the eligibility requirements and your 2025 return is assessed by June 19, 2026, your 2026 NOEC payments will be issued on the 10th of each month, starting on July 10, 2026.
If your 2025 return is assessed after June 19, 2026, your first payment, which will include any prior month entitlements, will be issued within four to eight weeks after your 2025 return is assessed. Your remaining payments will be issued on the 10th of each month thereafter. If the 10th of the month falls on a weekend or statutory holiday, the payment is issued on the last working day before the 10th of that month.
If you already receive your income tax refund or other benefits or credits by direct deposit, you will also receive your NOEC payments by direct deposit. Otherwise, you will receive your payments by cheque.
If, on your 2025 return, you chose to wait to get your 2026 OTB entitlement in one payment at the end of the benefit year and your annual entitlement is over $500, it will be issued on June 10, 2027. See question 9 under Ontario trillium benefit for more details.
If your 2026 OTB annual entitlement is $500 or less, it will be issued in one lump-sum payment in the first payment month (usually July).

## 9. Who will issue the 2026 NOEC payments?
The Canada Revenue Agency will issue the 2026 NOEC payments on behalf of the Ontario provincial government.

## 10. What is a principal residence?
A principal residence is a housing unit in Ontario that you usually occupy during the year. For example, it can be a house, apartment, condominium, hotel or motel room, mobile home, land-lease home*,  or rooming house.
*A land-lease home is a home you own but lease the lot it sits on. Land-lease homes are treated the same as mobile homes for the purposes of the OEPTC (see questions 11, 12, and 13).

## 11. What is eligible rent paid and what should I enter beside box 61100 on Form ON-BEN ?
Eligible rent paid is the rent that was paid for 2025 by or for you, for your principal residence, which was subject to Ontario municipal or school property tax. This means that if you lived, for example, in a social housing unit that was exempt from property tax, you may not enter rent paid for that unit in box 61100.
Beside box 61100, you should enter the total rent paid for your principal residence for 2025.
If you lived with your spouse or common-law partner on December 31, 2025, enter the total rent that was paid for both of you (including any amounts paid during a period of separation).
If you and your spouse or common-law partner separated during the year and lived apart on December 31, 2025 , enter your share of the rent for the part of the year before the separation, plus your own rent after the separation.
If you shared a principal residence with one or more persons (other than your spouse or common-law partner), enter your share of the rent paid by or for you for the year.
If a portion of the rent paid for your principal residence has been claimed as a business expense, this amount should be deducted from the total amount claimed at line 61100 for the year.
If you did not own your principal residence and it was not held in trust for you, and an amount for property tax was paid by or for you for that residence in place of your rent or in addition to your rent, enter the amount beside box 61100.
If you were a farmer, enter the rent paid for your principal residence and one acre of land beside box 61100.
If you lived in a mobile home or land-lease home:
·     If you rented the mobile home or land-lease home that you did not own, enter the amount of rent paid to your landlord beside box 61100.
·     If you owned the mobile home or land-lease home, do not enter any amount of rent paid to your landlord/site owner for the lot you leased for your home beside box 61100.
If you rented a mobile home that you did not own, enter the amount of rent paid to your landlord beside box 61100.
If you lived in a private long-term care home, hospital, group home, chronic care facility or other similar institution, and the institution paid full municipal and school property taxes, enter your rent beside box 61100. If the institution does not break down the cost of room and board (meals, laundering, or other services) on your receipt, you can claim an amount of up to 75% of your total payments as rent.
If you lived in a public or non-profit long-term care home, see question 15 .

## 12. What is eligible property tax and what should I enter beside box 61120 on Form ON-BEN ?
Eligible property tax is the amount of taxes paid for 2025 by or for you, for a home you own in Ontario .
Beside box 61120, you should enter your total property tax paid for your principal residence for 2025, including the municipal and school property taxes.
If you reside in an area that does not have a municipal organization (is not part of a municipality), you may include amounts charged under the Provincial Land Tax Act, or the Local Roads Boards Act .
If you lived with your spouse or common-law partner on December 31, 2025, enter the total property tax paid for both of you (including any amounts paid during a period of separation).
If you and your spouse or common-law partner separated during the year and lived apart on December 31, 2025 , enter your share of the property tax for the part of the year before the separation, plus your own property tax paid after the separation.
If you shared and jointly owned a principal residence with one or more persons (other than your spouse or common-law partner), enter your share of the property tax paid for the year.
If you were a farmer, enter the property tax paid for your principal residence located on the farm and one acre of land.
If you owned and occupied a mobile home or land-lease home, enter the combined total of the property tax paid for your home, plus the property tax that your landlord/site owner paid for the lot you leased. If the landlord/site owner does not provide a breakdown of the property tax paid for the lot, and you do not have an assessment for the lot from the Municipal Property Assessment Corporation, you can estimate the property tax based on the fees paid to your landlord/site owner for the lot. It would be reasonable to take 20% of the fees and add that amount to any property tax paid on the mobile home or land-lease home.

## 13. What amounts cannot be included as rent or property tax paid?
Your rent and property tax paid cannot include amounts such as:
- rent paid for a principal residence that is not subject to municipal and school property tax (such as a residence located on a reserve, or a tax-exempt social housing unit)
- other fees included in your rent that are for goods and services (for example, fees for board, housekeeping or other amenities)
- condo fees (other than any part that is paid for the municipal and school property tax for your principal residence)
- where you own and occupy a mobile home or land-lease home, rent paid to your landlord/site owner for the lot you leased for your mobile home or land-lease home (in this situation, enter only the property tax paid; however, if you do not know the amount of property tax paid for the lot you leased, you can enter, beside box 61120, 20% of fees that you paid for the lot, as mentioned in the answer to question 12 )
- payments to relatives or friends who are not reporting the payments as rental income on their returns
- property tax or rent paid on part of a home you used for rental or business purposes
- property tax or rent paid on a second residence, such as a cottage, if you claimed property tax or rent for your principal residence for the same period or
- amounts paid for living in a designated Ontario university, college or private school residence

## 14. What should I enter beside box 61210 on Form ON-BEN for my home energy costs paid on a reserve in Ontario for 2025?
If you lived on a reserve in Ontario, beside box 61210, you should enter the total of the amounts paid for 2025 for your energy costs (for example, electricity, heating) for your principal residence on the reserve.

## 15. What should I enter beside box 61230 on Form ON-BEN for the amounts I paid for living in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Ontario for 2025?
If you lived in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Ontario and the institution did not pay full municipal and school taxes, you should enter only the accommodation charges that were paid by or for you for 2025 beside box 61230.
If you lived in a private long-term care home, see question 11 .

## 16. Do I have to provide my receipts with my 2025 return to prove the amounts I paid?
No. Whether you are filing electronically or filing a paper return, keep all your receipts in case the CRA asks to see them at a later date.

## 17. My spouse owns our home but they are living in a nursing home. Are we entitled to the 2026 NOEC?
In a situation where for medical reasons spouses or common-law partners live apart and maintain separate principal residences in Northern Ontario on December 31, 2025, if they choose, each may apply for the 2026 NOEC for their separate eligible residences. For the purposes of these claims, the CRA considers you to be involuntarily separated, so your claims will be calculated as if you were single.
On your Form ON-BEN , Application for the 2026 Ontario Trillium Benefit and Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant , in addition to completing the application area for the NOEC, tick box 61080 in Part A on the back of the form and provide your spouse's or common-law partner's address in Part C – Involuntary separation. This will let the CRA know that there is an involuntary separation.

## 18. My spouse and I pay property taxes on both our house and our cottage. When we calculate our property tax paid, can we add together the amounts we paid for both properties?
No. You can only enter the property tax you paid on your principal residence in Ontario. You can only have one principal residence at a time. As well, only one of you can apply for the NOEC payments for both of you.

## 19. In the year of death, is the estate eligible for any NOEC payment(s) for that year?
If the deceased was single, separated, divorced or widowed and the date of death is before July 1, 2026, the estate is not eligible for any of the 2026 NOEC payments.
If the date of death is July 1, 2026, or later, the estate is only eligible for the 2026 NOEC payment(s) for a particular month if the date of death is on or after the first day of that month.
If the deceased had a spouse or common-law partner who had filed an income tax and benefit return and is eligible, the surviving spouse or common-law partner will automatically receive the remaining payments for the year in question. The income of both spouses/partners will continue to be used in the calculation of the NOEC.

## 20. How will the Canada Revenue Agency determine if I am a single parent so that I will qualify for the family-based 2026 NOEC payments?
The Canada Revenue Agency will consider you a single parent for a particular payment month, if you were not married or living in a common-law relationship on December 31, 2025, and, on the first day of that particular payment month, our benefit registry shows that you have care (including shared custody care) of a child under the age of 18.

## 21. My ex-spouse and I share custody of our child. Neither of us has a new spouse or common-law partner. Will I receive the 2026 NOEC payments for my child?
There are no NOEC payments for children. However, as long as the CRA considers you to be a single parent for a particular month (as described in the previous question), your payment for that month will be based on the family credit amount and income threshold (see question 6 ). If the CRA does not consider you to be a single parent for that month, your payment will be based on the single credit amount and income threshold (see question 6 ). If you are a single parent who shares the care of all your children for a payment issuance month, your payment would be calculated as the average of the family-based and single-based NOEC payments.

## 22. I'm single and I applied for the 2026 NOEC on my 2025 tax return. I still live in Northern Ontario and I gave birth to my first child on August 2, 2026. I do not share the care of my child. Will I qualify for the family-based NOEC payments?
For the July and August 2026 payments, you would only be entitled to the NOEC payment based on the single credit amount and income threshold (see question 6 ). However, after you register your child for the Canada child benefit (CCB), your remaining 2026 NOEC payments will be based on the family credit amount and income threshold (see question 6 ).

## 23. How do I register my child for the Canada child benefit (CCB) so that I will get the family-based 2026 NOEC payments?
To register your child, you can apply online on My Account by using the Apply for child benefits service or you can complete and send the CRA a Form RC66, Canada Child Benefits Application . You can download this form or order a copy. Go to forms and publications .
Send your completed form to the tax centre that serves your area.

## 24. I filed for bankruptcy in 2025. Can I apply for the 2026 NOEC payments?
Yes. Provided you meet the eligibility criteria explained in question 3 , you can apply for the 2026 NOEC payments. However, you would file your Form ON-BEN with the post-bankruptcy return you file for the tax year ending December 31, 2025. When the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) calculates your adjusted family net income, the CRA will include your net income for the pre- and post-bankruptcy periods.

## 25. I immigrated from outside of Canada to Northern Ontario on June 15, 2025. Can I apply for the 2026 NOEC payments?
Yes. Provided you meet the eligibility criteria explained in question 3 , you can apply for the 2026 NOEC payments. However, in addition to completing Form ON-BEN , you will also have to file Form RC66SCH, Status in Canada / Statement of Income , to state your income from January 1, 2025, to the date you entered Canada. If you are married or living in a common-law relationship and your spouse or common-law partner resides in Canada, you would also provide your spouse's or common-law partner's income for the same period on Form RC66SCH.

## 26. I have not filed my 2025 income tax and benefit return. Will I receive a 2026 NOEC payment?
No. To receive the 2026 NOEC amounts, you must file a 2025 income tax and benefit return and apply for the NOEC (see question 4 ).

## 27. I applied for the 2026 NOEC on my 2025 return and met all of the conditions to get these payments. However, I have not received any payments—what should I do?
You should wait until you have received your 2025 notice of assessment. Once you receive your 2025 notice, you can call 1-877-627-6645 and an agent will help you. The first 2026 NOEC payment is scheduled to be issued on July 10, 2026 .

## 28. I am eligible for the 2026 NOEC payments but I forgot to apply for them when I filed my 2025 income tax and benefit return. Can I still apply for them?
Yes, you can request adjustments for previous years, as long as they were relatively recent returns. For the 2026 NOEC, you have to request an adjustment to your 2025 return. Do not file another 2025 income tax and benefit return. Instead, you can make your request by using our My Account online service. My Account is a secure, convenient, and time-saving way to access and manage your tax and benefit information online, seven days a week. For information on how to register, see My Account .
If you haven't signed up for My Account, you can send one of the following items to your tax centre: a completed Form T1-ADJ, T1 Adjustment Request , or a signed letter indicating that you want to apply for the 2026 NOEC amounts on your 2025 return.
Please attach a completed Form ON-BEN to your letter or Form T1-ADJ or provide information for whichever of the following amounts apply to you:
- the rent paid by or for you for your principal residence in Ontario for 2025
- the property tax paid by or for you for your principal residence in Ontario for 2025
- the home energy costs paid by or for you for your principal residence on a reserve in Ontario for 2025 or
- the accommodation costs paid by or for you for living in a public or non-profit long-term care home in Ontario for 2025
You must also include your supporting receipts.
If you forgot to apply for the NOEC on an earlier return, you still can as long as it was a relatively recent return. Just follow the instructions above but provide the requested information for the applicable tax year.

## Related services and information

### Give feedback about this page
Thank you for your feedback.
